status: DRAFT — NOT PUBLISHED — SME REVIEW REQUIRED
note: DRAFT end-of-course assessment for GP-445 pilot (WS2b). This course carries IICRC CEC hours (7) and its live WordPress description advertises a 'Quiz' with no assessment currently seeded — see GP-444 evidence. Structured identically to data/seed/floor-care-quizzes.json (courseSlug binding, quiz array, questions/options/correctIndex). DO NOT seed or publish until the founder (SME) has reviewed each question against docs/runbooks/sme-review-checklist.md and ticked this course off. Source: data/Microbial.docx 'Infectious Control for the Business Owner' course record (20 modules) plus data/wordpress-export/courses.json live description (slug match confirmed). Every question includes a sourceRef note field citing the docx module for SME traceability — sourceRef is a review aid only and is not consumed by the seed script.

quizzes.lessonTitle: End-of-course assessment: Infectious Control for the Business Owner
quizzes.title: Infectious Control for the Business Owner — Final Assessment

questions.questionText: Why can a disinfectant that is effective against influenza fail against norovirus, even at the same site?
options: Norovirus is a non-enveloped virus and lacks the lipid membrane that many disinfectants target
Norovirus only lives in the air, not on surfaces
All disinfectants work identically against every virus type
Influenza is always harder to kill than norovirus
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 1: Virus Facts

questions.questionText: What available chlorine concentration do current Australian state health guidelines (e.g. Queensland Health, NSW Clinical Excellence Commission, SA Health) specify for cleaning surfaces where C. difficile is suspected or confirmed?
options: 100 ppm
500 ppm
1000 ppm
No chlorine-based product is effective against spores
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx Module 2 (Bacteria and Modes of Transmission); 1000 ppm verified 2026-07-05 against Queensland Health CDI guideline (2024), NSW CEC and SA Health (0.1% = 1000 ppm). NHMRC prints no ppm; ACIPC/ASID notes 5000 mg/L for consistent sporicidal efficacy — stem reframed to ask what AU guidance specifies, not a universal spore-kill claim. SME to ratify.

questions.questionText: A technician is about to disinfect a visibly soiled high-touch surface. What is the compliant sequence?
options: Apply disinfectant directly to the soiled surface to save time
Clean to physically remove organic matter first, then disinfect
Sterilise the surface instead of disinfecting it
Disinfect twice instead of cleaning
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 3: Cleaning and Disinfecting for Health

questions.questionText: Which regulatory requirement governs the disinfectant products a business can legally claim as effective against pathogens in Australia?
options: Products must be registered with the Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA)
Any commercially available cleaning product may be marketed as a disinfectant
Only imported products require registration
State health departments issue their own separate disinfectant licences
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 3: Cleaning and Disinfecting for Health (Compliance note)

questions.questionText: Why should jewellery such as rings and watches be removed before a worker enters a contaminated zone?
options: It is a uniform policy with no hygiene basis
It impedes effective hand hygiene and can harbour and transport pathogens between sites
It slows the worker down physically
It has no bearing on infection control
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 4: Hygiene Practices

questions.questionText: Why does infection control work in an aged care or childcare setting carry a heightened duty of care compared to a standard commercial job?
options: These sites pay less, so more care must be taken to justify cost
Vulnerable populations mean a failure carries significantly higher regulatory and legal consequences
There is no difference in duty of care between site types
These sites are exempt from WHS regulation
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 5: Understanding Risks

questions.questionText: As a business owner, what is your personal legal exposure under the WHS Act if your infection control teams are inadequately trained, equipped, or supervised?
options: None — liability sits only with the individual worker
You may be personally liable as an officer for failing to exercise due diligence over WHS systems
Liability only applies to publicly listed companies
Liability transfers entirely to the client once the contract is signed
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 5: Understanding Risks (Compliance note)

questions.questionText: What document should every client receive that specifies surfaces cleaned, method, chemical and dilution, and frequency?
options: A verbal summary at the end of the job
A written cleaning schedule, which is also the primary defence against a claim that cleaning was not performed as agreed
No documentation is required if the client trusts the business
Only the invoice
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 6: Cleaning Types and Touch Points

questions.questionText: What is the minimum thermal disinfection standard given for reusable microfibre before it is stored and reused?
options: 40 degrees Celsius for 5 minutes
60 degrees Celsius minimum for 10 minutes
Cold wash only
No thermal standard is specified; any wash cycle is acceptable
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 7: Cross Contamination

questions.questionText: A prospective client wants to engage your business for a confirmed outbreak response job. What should you confirm first regarding insurance?
options: That your standard general public liability policy automatically covers all biohazard work
Whether your policy has sub-limits or exclusions for infection control, mould remediation, or biohazard response, and confirm coverage in writing
Insurance is not relevant to infection control contracts
Only workers compensation needs to be checked
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 8: Insurance and Liability

questions.questionText: A client rings to report a 'routine clean' following a suspected COVID exposure. What must the business do before finalising the job scope?
options: Accept the client's phone description as the final scope
Conduct a physical site inspection, since the site may in fact present as a higher-category biohazard scene
Quote based on the phone call only to save time
Refuse the job outright without inspection
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 10: Hazard Identification

questions.questionText: Under Model WHS Act s.19, what is the primary evidence that a PCBU has actively managed risks so far as is reasonably practicable for a specific infection control job?
options: A verbal assurance from the site supervisor
A documented, site-specific risk assessment for that job
The absence of any prior complaints
A general company safety policy with no job-specific detail
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 11: Risk Assessments

questions.questionText: When scoping an infection control job, in what order should the Hierarchy of Controls be applied?
options: PPE first, then work backwards to other controls
Elimination and substitution and engineering and administrative controls worked through first, with PPE addressing only the residual risk
All controls are equally weighted and can be applied in any order
PPE is the only control that matters for biological hazards
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 12: Hierarchy of Controls

questions.questionText: Under Model WHS Regulations r.344, who must be provided the SDS for a hazardous chemical used on an infection control job?
options: Only the team leader
Every worker likely to be affected by the hazardous chemical
Only the business owner
The client only, on request
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 13: Safety Data Sheets

questions.questionText: Under Model WHS Regulations r.342, does a hazardous chemical decanted into a spray bottle on site require a label?
options: It is exempt only if the chemical is used immediately and the container is thoroughly cleaned after use so no residue remains; otherwise it must be labelled
No container of a hazardous chemical ever requires a label
It must always be labelled regardless of how it is used
It is exempt as long as it is used within the same work shift
questions.sourceRef: Model WHS Regulations 2011 r.342 (Labelling hazardous chemicals—containers), verified 2026-07-05 against legislation.gov.au F2011L02664. Corrects the earlier r.330 citation and the answer: r.330 is manufacturer SDS preparation, and r.342's decant/immediate-use exemption means the previous flat 'always label regardless of duration' key was legally wrong. SME to ratify.

questions.questionText: How often must workers sign off that they have read and understood the relevant infection control SOPs?
options: Once, at initial induction only
Before starting each infection control job, not as a one-time induction step
Only after an incident occurs
Sign-off is not required if the worker has prior experience
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 15: Standard Operating Procedures

questions.questionText: What triggers a review of a JSEA already in progress on an infection control job?
options: JSEAs are fixed for the life of the job and cannot be updated
Discovery of additional contaminated areas, a change in identified pathogen, a change in cleaning method, or a PPE failure/exposure incident
Only the end of the working day
Only a written request from the client
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 16: Job Safety and Environmental Analysis (JSEA)

questions.questionText: Under Model WHS Regulations r.299, when must a SWMS for high-risk infection control work (e.g. confined space entry) be prepared?
options: At any point during the job, including after the fact for record-keeping
Before the high-risk construction work commences — a retroactively prepared SWMS provides no legal protection
Only if requested by the regulator during an inspection
SWMS are optional for infection control businesses
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 17: Safe Work Method Statements (SWMS)

questions.questionText: What does RLU (Relative Light Units) measure in an ATP hygiene verification protocol?
options: The temperature of the disinfected surface
The bioluminescent reaction between residual ATP and the luciferin-luciferase reagent, indicating incomplete removal of biological material
The concentration of chlorine remaining on the surface
The number of workers who touched the surface
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 18: ATP Terminology

questions.questionText: Before fixing a pass/fail RLU threshold for a new client site or surface type, what does the course recommend?
options: Set the threshold immediately from a single test result
Collect baseline ATP data over at least three cleaning cycles before fixing a threshold, since single-point data is not statistically reliable
Use the same threshold for every site regardless of sector
Skip threshold-setting entirely and rely on visual inspection
questions.sourceRef: Microbial.docx — Infectious Control for the Business Owner, Module 20: ATP and Protocols
